speaker: interviewer
text: Interview with Felicia Carmelly, maiden name Steigman, tape 2.

speaker: interviewee
text: I just like to finish about my maternal grandmother with the fact-- and her family, the three sisters. Their family name was Siegler. And I want to talk a little bit about my father's parents. My grandfather's name was Elchanan Steigman, and my grandmother's name was Beile Steigman. And they-- we lived in the same house, which had the four apartments, and different apartments. But the relationship between them and my mother was not the best, which had an impact on me. And I imagine that it was not the best, because my mother didn't bring the dowry that they expected. Nevertheless, we lived in the house. And in the backyard of the house, my grandparents had built a steady sukkah, which had a roof which opened up with a big stick. And that was another very joyous holiday, because I loved to go and decorate the sukkah with my friends with leaves, and pine cones, and all kinds of fruit and stuff. And sometimes, because we lived in the mountains, it was pretty cold to eat in the sukkah. But even if we had to wear sweaters, or even if we have to a winter coat, that was it. I mean, I would-- we would not eat anywhere else. It was the sukkah for the whole week. And that's another very fond memory that, that I have from my childhood. [CLEARS THROAT] Now, they had-- my paternal grandparents-- had three children. One, one's name was Rachel, and people say that I look like her. I never met her. She died before I was born. Another was Eti, and she was with us in the camps. She now lives in Israel. And another is my father, who was the oldest, Elchanan Steigman. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So back to 1940, '41, when things started getting worse and worse for the Jewish people in town.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when we had to wear the yellow star, and when there were curfews and you weren't allowed to do a myriad of things. But they didn't impact on me that much, because I was too small to, to be in the adult world, which was restricted. However, I do remember some incidents. One of them which had a significant impact on me was-- oh, we also had to close the, the windows, the glass. Anything that was glass in the house, windows or doors, had to be closed so that the light cannot be seen outside, because there were Soviet Russian planes and something. So that was-- everything was camouflaged. We weren't allowed to go out on the street late. And one day, there was an announcement that all the men had to gather in the building of the temple. [CLEARS THROAT] And they were holding-- the Iron Guard was holding the men hostage until-- I don't remember how-- what quantity of money and gold and silver is delivered to them from the community. And they threatened that if that will not be done, then they will burn the building with the men in the building. That was, of course-- that was the first big threat, the first big physical threat. There were psychological threats before, listening to the radio, listening to what's happening in other places, or anti-Semitic comments, or, or gestures, or swearing. But this was the first physical threat to the community, the fact that all the men were taken and locked up in the synagogue. They were not being given water. They were not being given food. [CLEARS THROAT]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I think it was the second day or maybe the third day, my mother decided to go and see if they're still alive. And it was walking distance. It was about 10 minutes. The temple was about 10 minutes away from where we lived. And the streets were with cobblestones.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother put a flashlight in her, in her purse so she could flash the light into the windows and see what's happening inside in the synagogue. And since there was no one to leave me with, she told me to put my coat on. And I marched with her to the synagogue. Well, we saw the men. Some were lying on the floor. Some were sitting on the benches. They were unshaven. They were dirty. They were hungry. They were afraid. It was a very threatening sight. And when we-- on the way-- and we cried. And we didn't know what will happen to them. [CLEARS THROAT] And somehow on the way back, it started to get dark. And my mother took out her flashlight, pointing it to the road to-- so that we can not trip over the cobblestones. And suddenly a soldier caught us and accused us of signaling to the Soviet planes. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So my mother emptied the contents of her purse into his pockets, and-- and he let us go home.

speaker: interviewee
text: [CLEARS THROAT] The day after that, my Uncle Armin managed to, to smuggle out from the synagogue, because there were no non-Jewish bakers in town. And there was no bread. So they allowed the bakers to come out so that they prepare the bread. And my uncle Armin pretended to be a baker. And he came out and-- well, he helped the bakers for a while and then eventually, I don't know if whatever they-- there demands were, [? worries ?] or not, but eventually the men were let out. And I guess because I was so young, I really don't remember the flow of the whole story. I remember separate incidents from the story. But I, I don't exactly know when this happened, and when the other happened, and how they all fit together.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the next thing I remember was announcements at that time. Since not many people had radios-- or a telephone, of course, was unheard of-- the announcements from the city hall were made by somebody coming out on the stairs of the city hall and drumming.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that meant that the population has to gather around, and they would make all kinds of announcements. Announcements about wearing the yellow star, or about curfews, or about what have you. And in October-- [CLEARS THROAT] OK, my birthday is in September. And I turned 10 in September '41. And I expected it to be as it usually was, a lot of fun, and a lot of gifts, and the-- the party, and all of that. But that was not the case. As a matter of fact, some of my, my aunts even forgot to buy me presents. And I knew there was something very wrong. I didn't know what, what it is, but I knew that there's something very wrong. And a short while after that, about a week or 10 days after that, the drummer made the announcement that all Jews from the city have to gather in the afternoon at the train station. And we have to take with us only what we can carry and food for three days. [CLEARS THROAT] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We-- the adults knew that other communities had been deported before us. But somehow-- one of the big tragedies of the war was that Jewish people didn't believe what's going to happen to them, or that the Germans could really do that as such a civilized country, or that the Romanians, with whom we were friends until a year ago, could do that to us. So-- everybody assumed that since we were told to take food for three days, that wherever they're taking us, we're going to be back in three days. So everybody-- my-- my parents packed little suitcases and rucksacks for everybody. And I had my rucksack, which I used to take for mountain climbing, except that it was packed. And this time it was very, very, very heavy. And also I was dressed with everything, double or three. Like, three pair of panties, three pair of t-shirts. They weren't called t-shirts. I forgot what they were called. And two sweaters and two coats. And like, I could hardly move. And the rucksack was packed really tight, so it was pretty heavy. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything special that you took to put in your rucksack?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I don't remember. The rucksack itself was important to me. But I don't remember what-- no I didn't take anything. I didn't take anything, because my mother packed it with practical things. [CLEARS THROAT] And there wasn't any room for anything else. [CLEARS THROAT] Anyway, so we went to the train station, and I had been on trains before. We went to visit my aunt's city and we traveled to Bucharest. And I, I enjoyed being on a train. There was a conductor, and they came to check the tickets. And it was nice. I was sitting with my mother. She was reading me books. And I was walking around, I was looking out. It was a really nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: So several passenger trains passed by, and none of them stopped for us. So I didn't know what we were waiting there for in the rain. And it was getting pretty cold. And then suddenly this long, long, very long freight train rolled along, and it stopped.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they started loading us into the freight train. And that was-- people started to scream, and to cry, and to become hysterical. [SIGHS] And people got lost. Families got lost. Children got lost from their parents, and parents were screaming to get their kids back. And, and the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they had put some platforms to the opening so that people can walk up, but it was slippery. And it was muddy, and it was raining. People fell, and eventually-- it took a long time to load that train. It took a long time, because every box car was filled with 60 to 80 people. And there was standing room only. And on top of that, the floor had manure. [CLEARS THROAT]

speaker: interviewer
text: So everybody was-- even when you were already in the, in the, in the car, you were slipping all over the place, because of the manure and the mud that we brought in. So eventually after the train was loaded, we-- it stood for awhile.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then the locomotive was-- it was-- trains, at that time were with steam something. I remember the noise of the steam coming out of the locomotive. And then it just blew its whistle, and the train took off. And we didn't know where. And then before the train took off, they closed the, the doors, and they sealed the doors from outside. And every box car had one or two gendarmes. I don't actually know where they were on the outside, but they were in charge with that particular box car. And, and then when we were inside, as soon as they closed the doors, people felt that there wasn't enough air to breathe. And there was a little opening-- [CLEARS THROAT] --with a grill, which was an opening for when animals are being transported for some air to come in. And everybody scrambled, and everybody wanted to sit there because that-- there was more air there. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So the people got angry at each other and started pushing one another. It was-- the process of dehumanization started immediately after embarking that train.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was standing-- I was probably as tall as-- my head was reaching the waist of the adults So there was even less air for me. And I actually experimented with trying to lean, because there were people all around me. So I was leaning all over the place to see how far I can lean. I couldn't lean very far. And at some point I slipped, but I couldn't fall, because there was not room for me to fall. And then I think what, what had a big impact on me was that it was dark all the time. So I didn't know when it was daytime outside or when it was night, because it was dark. Where I was down there, it was dark all the time. And only the people who were near that opening, near the grill, would know when it's daytime and when it's nighttime. There were, of course, no toilets. So everybody eliminated on the spot, on themselves, which was another source of great suffering, particularly for people like my mother. And the whole town, I think, was compulsively clean.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I, I asked my mother. I said, I need to go to the bathroom. She says, go. And I couldn't believe that she said that. I said, well, where should I go? She says, wherever you can. And the only place I could was in my pants. And that was, it was just a terrible feeling.

speaker: interviewee
text: We, we were not human anymore. And people were scrambling for food. They, they couldn't find their, their rucksacks or bags that were sitting in the manure. They couldn't find which is theirs, because it was dark. And they wanted some food or some people brought-- were smart enough to bring water. And they didn't open the, the doors. And eventually, my mother talked my father into bribing one of the gendarmes to open the door for a few minutes to get some air in. So he gave him his gold watch, which I remember was Schaffhausen, and at that time, that was the most fancy watch that one could have. Gold too. And I think he also gave him a fountain pen or something else. He gave him two objects to open the door. [CLEARS THROAT] And he opened the door and let some air in. But five minutes after, he closed it. The stink was back, and, and there was no air. And then I remember people getting into-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --a fight because there was a young woman with a baby in our car. And she had-- the baby was hungry, and she had no milk. She either didn't have enough food or drink or what have you. Anyway, she wasn't producing milk, and the baby was very hungry. And the baby was crying. And the crying of the baby, under those circumstances, went on people's nerves. And people started to, to yell at her to shut up the baby. And she didn't know what to do. And eventually she was under so much pressure that she took a brooch that she had on her coat and pricked her nipple to let the blood come, and she put the baby to her breast to suck the blood. Things like this, which I dream about still today and which show me today-- I didn't understand it then-- how quickly that process of becoming inhuman happened. [CLEARS THROAT] Eventually, I think it was after three days and three nights, we arrived in a place called Ataki, which was in Bessarabia. It was a border town-- used to be before the Germans and the Romanians invaded the Ukraine, it was a border town on the River Dniester. And across this town was a larger city, called Mogilev, which was on the Russian side-- on Ukrainian territory. And they-- the unloading of the train was also a very traumatic experience, because everything had to be done fast. And they were shouting, and we were slipping. And we were full of manure and full of our own excrement. And the-- and, and we ended up in this large field in the rain. I think there was some walking to do to this field, but I don't remember that part that well. From the train to the field, I think there was some walking. But I remember the field where there were a lot of people who had been deported before us, and they'd already been in this field for a number of days. And you slept in the mud, and there was no food. And these-- as bad as we looked, those people looked a lot worse, because not only had they been brought there in trains the same as we were, but on top of that, they slept in the mud for several days. And there, and there was no food, and they were cold. And the clothes, and the shoes, everything was, was mud. And, and they looked like bewildered people. And there were people from-- a lot of them were people from Bessarabia. And my mom and I visited my aunt in Bessarabia before, and, and the people were elegant. And they wore hats, and they were-- and I couldn't understand what they're doing in this field.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, why did they leave their homes? Why did they leave their gorgeous clothes and their gorgeous hats? And what are they doing here? And what's happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was-- it was-- everything became unknown, from the way life used to be before when we knew exactly what every minute and every day will bring. Suddenly, every minute was in a total unknown thing. And not only did we not know what's going to be, but we knew that it's going to be bad. Whatever we didn't know, we knew it's going to be bad. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Eventually, after-- yeah, and eventually after a few days of, of us staying in this field too, they brought barges. Dniester is a very big, fast, deep river. And we had to cross the Dniester to get to the other side, to the Ukraine, where they were deporting a large segment of the population from Romania, particularly from Bukovina and Bessarabia. And my town was in the Province of Bukovina.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Bessarabia and Bukovina, all of them were-- most of them were in the field, other than those who managed to escape with the Russians eastwards, when the Russians withdrew from the Germans or men in arm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Which, by the way, my Aunt [INAUDIBLE], her husband and her baby did. They managed to escape with the Russians deeper into the Soviet Union. Unfortunately, in one of the train stations where they went down for water, she was getting very tired. She had a one-year-old son. She was getting tired holding the baby, and she gave the baby to her husband to hold the baby. And there was a, a bombing of the train, and her husband and the baby were killed in front of her eyes. And the rest of the people who remained alive were shoved into the train. And the train took off back to the end of Russia, where she spent, with her mother-in-law, four years all by herself. I mean, she and her mother-in-law, working hard in the-- but at least there was no danger from the Nazis there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we crossed the Dniester on a barge into a territory which was called by the Germans, at that time, Transnistria, meaning on the other side of the river Dniester. This name did not exist on a map. The Germans and the Romanians made it up, and it only existed for four years.

speaker: interviewee
text: When-- when the territory was reconquered by the Russians in 1944, the name of this large territory disappeared. And that's a territory as big as Nova Scotia. It's 16,000 square miles, with hundreds of villages and towns, all of which became camps. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And all of which were, by the way, inhabited by many local Ukrainian Jews. But their life was a lot more primitive. It was-- it was communism. We lived in so-called capitalism and monarchy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So our life was more advanced, so to speak, materially. And they lived in a lot more primitive conditions. And we were shoved into the huts with, with them. A lot of the men had been taken to the Russian army, so the population we found there were women, old men, old women, and kids. So on this barge when the crossed the Dniester, I happened to be standing beside this woman who was in our car. And she was holding the baby in one arm and the bundle of, I don't know, blankets or diapers or whatever-- of [GERMAN] in the other hand.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she was staring into the waves. And at some point she dropped the baby in the waves, and there wasn't a sound from her or from anybody else. And my mother, who was in the back of me, said to my father, look at what animals we became only in a few days. Nobody's jumping in to get the baby. It-- it was-- we never found out if she wanted to dump the baby or if she wanted to dump the bundle because it was to heavy for her. But nobody moved, nobody screamed, nobody said, oh, my god. Look what's happening.

speaker: interviewer
text: It-- What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the train when people died-- and there were people who died in the boxcar-- that is when I encountered death and saw dead people for the first time. But I didn't know that they were dead. I thought they were asleep, even though their faces change when they're dead. I was wondering how come their faces change when they're asleep. And I was wondering if my face changes like that when I'm asleep too. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I forgot one incident, which I think is important for both historic and personal reasons, which is before we were deported, my father was very well known in town. And he has a lot of friends, Jewish friends and not Jewish friends. And a lot of the people who bought in his store were very grateful to him, because he loaned them money, or he gave them on credit, or what have you. And one of the people who was a Legionnaire, a member of the Iron Guard, which was-- who were ferocious antisemites, came to my father's store one day and told him to pack the family and leave to Bucharest as soon as possible, because we're going to be deported. He was drunk when he came into the store. Maybe that's why he told my father, because that was supposed to be a secret. He was drunk, and he had a beer bottle in his, in his hands when he came into the store. And he told my father that, and my father started laughing. And he said no, that will never happen to us. And the guy got so angry that he punched my father with the beer bottle, hit him in the face. And my father came home with a split lip. And my mother-- they had to call the doctor to-- to stitch it together or what have you. Because he was furious that my father doesn't listen to him. That's how non-believing we were about what is expecting us. However, I think that my father did manage to send some money out to some of our friends who used to be tourists, who lived in Bucharest, some money that, that he had from the store. He managed to send some money out there. And then when we were in the camps in Transnistria, we got some of that money-- some of that money-- through some soldiers who were paid a fortune to bring the money. But I think that was helpful. So-- and that was as a result of this guy warning my father to leave. Anyway, so then we got to-- on the other side of the Dniester.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to ask you, how did you react as 10-year-old, 11-year-old, when you saw this woman throw her baby into the water?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I think that I was as hardened as the, as the adults were. I don't remember any particular feeling of sadness. Or-- I was not-- I remember one thing. I wasn't thinking about the baby. I was thinking about her. I looked right at her, because she was standing right beside me, and her face was like a statue. It was like she was not alive. She was staring into the water, and she did that before she dropped the baby, and she did that after she dropped the baby. There wasn't a tear, and there wasn't a move on our face at all. She was like a stone face. And, and that's what impacted on me, somehow more than the baby. I don't know why, but that-- that was-- that image remained forever in my head.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll go to break now.
